title: The judge of the 2016 Nice terror attack trial reflects on 'abominable' case, 10 years after
subhead: Laurent Raviot presided over the Paris special criminal court that heard the trial of the attack in Nice that killed 86 people on July 14, 2016. On the tenth anniversary of the attack, he granted Le Monde his first interview, speaking with unusual candor.

title: 2026 World Cup: Ahead of semifinal against Spain, France's unity is its superpower
France celebrates Bastille Day, Macron's last as president, with parade showcasing unity with allies and Ukraine
Trump backtracks on plan to toll ships in Strait of Hormuz
subhead: United States President Donald Trump scrapped a plan to impose a levy on ships moving through the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, citing discussions with Middle East partners. He said he would continue to bar passage of all Iranian ships.

title: Macron and Zelensky, a special relationship marked by misunderstandings
subhead: Ukrainian soldiers will join France's annual Bastille Day military parade in Paris on Tuesday, July 14. French President Emmanuel Macron and his Ukrainian counterpart Volodymyr Zelensky will stand together in a sign of Paris's support for Kyiv, but their relationship has not always been so straightforward.

title: 'Immigration is beneficial for the economy as a whole. Why is it so unpopular?'
subhead: A new study highlights the economic benefits of immigration in OECD countries. These findings, which have been repeated for decades, unfortunately do not shift the debate, says Eric Albert in his column.

title: 'Les Bleus represent France because France entrusted them with its place and they accepted it'
subhead: As players of the French national football team are targeted by racist remarks, political scientist and writer Rachid Benzine argues confusion has arisen over what representation means, contending that it is not the same as resemblance.

subhead: The deals that US President Donald Trump so readily boasts about seemed destined to get bogged down in the Middle East right from the start.

title: 'And I thought coke was for stressed-out executives!': Cocaine's ravages among manual workers
subhead: 'Cocaine at work' (3/3). Long associated with elite, urban circles, cocaine has now made its way deep into rural France and its mid-sized towns. On construction sites, farms and in warehouses, workers, some barely out of their teens, are turning to the drug to get through the physical toll of their jobs.

title: Martine Barrat, a photographer without fear or limits
subhead: At 93, the artist has lost none of her vitality or curiosity. She lives at the legendary Hotel Chelsea in New York, where she remains one of the last year-round artist residents. Today, her work is the subject of a retrospective at the Rencontres d'Arles photography festival.
